metadata.summary: Build GraphQL APIs with schema-first SDL, per-request DataLoader for N+1 prevention, depth/complexity limits, cursor pagination, and graphql-inspector CI diffs.

content.title: GraphQL API
content.summary: A methodology for building GraphQL APIs: schema-first SDL development with codegen, per-request DataLoader for every 1:N relation, depth limit (5-8) and query complexity cap before resolvers run, cursor-based (Relay) pagination for any list, and graphql-inspector diff in CI to block breaking schema changes. Mutations return the modified entity, never Boolean. Authorization checks live per-resolver, not just at the gateway.

why.p: Without DataLoader, nested resolvers fire one DB query per parent object (N+1). Without depth and complexity limits, a single deeply nested query can DoS the server. Schema-first codegen ensures resolvers and clients share a single type definition. graphql-inspector catches breaking changes before they reach external consumers who cannot roll back. Returning entities from mutations enables optimistic UI updates; returning Boolean makes rollback impossible.

list.item: Mobile + web clients with divergent data shapes against the same backend
Aggregating 3+ microservices into one client-facing API (federation/stitching)
Internal tooling where consumers need fast schema iteration without REST versioning
Real-time UIs needing subscriptions over WebSocket / SSE
Backend-for-frontend (BFF) layers where type-safe client codegen is needed
File-upload-heavy APIs — use multipart REST or presigned URLs instead
Public APIs where CDN URL-level caching is critical — REST wins
Simple CRUD with one consumer — REST ships faster
Teams without query-cost / depth limiting infrastructure — DoS risk is real

section.name: schema-and-resolvers

rule.statement: Author the SDL schema first, run codegen to produce typed resolvers and clients, then implement resolvers. Never implement resolvers before the schema is committed — codegen output becomes the contract between frontend and backend.
rule.rationale: Schema-first ensures the client contract is explicit before any code is written. Resolver-first development produces schemas that are shaped by implementation accidents rather than domain requirements.

rule.statement: Instantiate DataLoaders inside the request context, never at module scope. One DataLoader instance per request per entity type. Module-level DataLoaders cache results across users and requests, leaking data between tenants.
rule.rationale: A module-level DataLoader for User records caches user A's data and may serve it to user B's request. Per-request scoping isolates caches to a single authenticated session.

example.title: module-level DataLoader
per-request DataLoader

section.reference: Per-request DataLoader context factory with batch functions

rule.statement: Use cursor-based (Relay) pagination for any list field that may return more than 20 items. Never return raw arrays for paginated resources — raw arrays block incremental loading and make infinite scroll impossible.
rule.rationale: Offset pagination is O(n) at the DB level and produces gaps/duplicates when items are inserted mid-page. Cursor pagination is stable and efficient at any depth.

example.title: Relay cursor pagination schema

rule.statement: Mutations must return the modified entity, not Boolean. Boolean returns make client-side cache invalidation impossible and force an additional refetch round-trip.
rule.rationale: Apollo Client and Relay rely on the returned entity to update their caches optimistically. A Boolean return provides no information for cache update; the client must re-query the entire resource.

example.title: mutation returning Boolean
mutation returning payload

section.reference: graphql-codegen config for TypeScript types and typed document nodes

rule.statement: Enforce a query depth limit (5-8 levels) and complexity cap (e.g., 1000 units) before any resolver runs. Both limits must be middleware, not per-resolver checks. An absence of either allows a single malicious query to DoS the server.
rule.rationale: Without depth limits, a query can request arbitrarily deep nested relationships, causing N^depth DB queries. Complexity caps prevent unbounded list traversals like querying all users' all orders' all items.

rule.statement: Implement authorization per-resolver or via a @auth directive on schema fields. Never rely solely on gateway-level auth — a resolver reachable from an unguarded query path leaks data even when the gateway blocks direct access.
rule.rationale: GraphQL queries can reach any field from multiple paths. A field accessible via an unrelated query traversal bypasses per-route gateway auth entirely.

rule.statement: Run graphql-inspector diff against the production schema on every PR. Fail the build on any breaking change that lacks an explicit migration note. Use @deprecated(reason: "use X instead") for field removal, removing only after analytics confirm zero usage.
rule.rationale: GraphQL has no built-in versioning. A field removal or type change silently breaks all clients using that field. graphql-inspector makes breaking changes an explicit, reviewable decision.

rule.statement: Enable persisted queries in production: clients send a hash, the server resolves the SDL. This eliminates arbitrary query injection DoS and slashes payload bandwidth by 60-90%.
rule.rationale: Ad-hoc query execution allows any authenticated user to submit maximally expensive queries. Persisted queries lock the server to known, pre-analyzed query shapes.

antipattern.reason: Caches results from one request and serves them to subsequent requests, leaking data between users. Per-request instantiation is mandatory.
subscriptions-transport-ws is unmaintained and has known issues. The current standard is graphql-ws. Many server libraries still default to the old protocol — opt in explicitly.

antipattern.description: Input types with nested optional objects creating impossible validation shapes.
antipattern.reason: Deeply nested optional inputs produce 2^n possible shapes that are impossible to validate exhaustively. Use flat, single-purpose input types: CreateOrderInput, UpdateOrderStatusInput.

antipattern.description: Adding a new resolver field without an accompanying DataLoader for 1:N traversal.
antipattern.reason: Every parent object triggers a separate DB query. 100 user nodes in a list query = 100 separate order queries. Always require an XByYLoader for any 1:N field.

antipattern.description: Mixing schema-first and code-first approaches in the same service without documentation.
antipattern.reason: Codegen output drift between the two approaches causes type mismatches that surface only at runtime. Pick one approach and document it in CLAUDE.md.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Adopt schema-first development: agent edits *.graphql SDL, runs codegen (graphql-codegen or server-language equivalent), implements resolvers, then a review subagent runs graphql-inspector diff against the production schema and fails on breaking changes without an explicit migration note. Pair with persisted-query enforcement and a query-cost analyzer; the agent must include cost annotations on new fields and the gate rejects unbounded list returns.

Recommended subagents: general-purpose subagent for schema authoring and resolver implementation; feature-executor for pipeline: edit SDL → codegen → impl resolvers → write integration tests → run schema diff; sdd-execution gate that enforces DataLoader presence, depth limit, and cost limit middleware on every server boot; code-review subagent for diff-based check for new resolvers without auth and DataLoader.
section.prompt: Add a `userOrders(first: Int, status: OrderStatus): OrderConnection!` field on User. 1. Edit schema/user.graphql with the connection type + edges. 2. Generate types: `pnpm graphql-codegen`. 3. Implement resolver in src/resolvers/user.ts using a per-request orderByUserLoader. 4. Wire authorization: only owner or admin can list orders. 5. Add Vitest test that asserts N=1 db query for 100 users. 6. Run `graphql-inspector diff origin/main schema.graphql`; output the report.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: graphql-codegen: Generate TS/Go/Python types from SDL — npm i -D @graphql-codegen/cli
graphql-inspector: Schema diff, breaking change detection — npm i -g @graphql-inspector/cli
rover (Apollo): Schema registry + federation composition — https://www.apollographql.com/docs/rover
graphqurl (gq): Curl-equivalent for GraphQL — npm i -g graphqurl
Altair / Insomnia: GraphQL client for manual queries — desktop apps
graphql-eslint: Lint queries + SDL — npm i -D @graphql-eslint/eslint-plugin
apollo-cost-analysis / graphql-query-complexity: Query-cost guards — server middleware
genql: Type-safe TS client from SDL — npm i genql

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Per-request DataLoader scoping: instantiate inside the request context, never module-level — module-level caches leak across users
Depth limit (5–8) + complexity limit (e.g., 1000 cost units) on the server, before resolvers run
Persisted queries in production: clients send a hash, server resolves SDL — kills query-injection DoS and slashes bandwidth
Connection-style pagination (Relay cursor) for any list >20 items; do not return raw arrays for tables
Authorization in a directive (@auth(requires: ROLE)) or per-resolver guard, never in the gateway alone
Versioning: never bump v1→v2; deprecate fields with @deprecated(reason: "...") and remove after analytics show 0 use
Subscriptions over graphql-ws (modern) not subscriptions-transport-ws (deprecated) — server libs default to old; agents must opt in
Mutations return the modified entity + clientMutationId; never return Boolean — clients can't refetch optimistically

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Agents emit field resolvers without DataLoader because tutorials skip it — always require an XByYLoader for any 1:N traversal
LLMs over-nest input types creating impossible-to-validate shapes; flatten to single-purpose CreateEntityActionInput!
Schema-first vs code-first: codegen output drift between the two confuses agents reading half the codebase. Pick one; document the choice in CLAUDE.md
Agents copy the original subscriptions-transport-ws from old docs; current standard is graphql-ws — pin the protocol explicitly
N+1 detection is not free — wire apollo-tracing or OpenTelemetry GraphQL instrumentation and assert tracing is on in tests
Human-in-loop checkpoint required when: making any breaking schema change, adding a federation @key, raising depth/cost limits in production
Token waste: do not paste the entire schema into context; rely on graphql-inspector summary + the touched type only
Agents forget that Boolean! non-null returns from mutations make rollback impossible; insist on entity returns

section.name: services
section.p: Apollo Studio / GraphOS (SaaS, Rover CLI): Schema registry, persisted queries, perf tracing
Hasura (OSS + SaaS, CLI + REST admin API): Auto-generates GraphQL from Postgres; agents drive via hasura migrate
WunderGraph / Cosmo (OSS): Federation router, agent-driven config
The Guild / GraphQL Mesh (OSS): Stitches REST/gRPC into GraphQL
AWS AppSync (SaaS, CDK): Managed GraphQL with VTL/JS resolvers
pothos / nexus (OSS libs): Code-first schema for TS — friendlier to LLM codegen than SDL-first
gqlgen (Go, OSS): SDL-first Go server with codegen

example.title: Minimal codegen.yml
example.code: # codegen.yml schema: 'src/schema/**/*.graphql' documents: 'src/**/*.{ts,tsx,graphql}' generates: src/__generated__/types.ts: plugins: [typescript, typescript-resolvers] config: contextType: '../context#GraphQLContext' mappers: User: '../db/user#UserRecord' Order: '../db/order#OrderRecord' src/__generated__/operations.ts: plugins: [typescript-operations, typed-document-node] hooks: afterAllFileWrite: [prettier --write]

template.purpose: graphql-codegen config generating TS types + typed document nodes
Per-request DataLoader instantiation inside GraphQL context
